pitch_rationale: The homes at this Spring, TX property display classic Texas suburban hip-roof construction. Shadow lengths cast from eave edges onto the ground are moderate — neither the short stubs of a 4:12 nor the elongated projections of an 8:12+ roof — pointing to a mid-range slope. The ratio of visible roof surface (nadir eave-to-ridge distance) to overall building footprint is consistent with a 6:12 pitch: the ridge lines are clearly defined but occupy a meaningful fraction of the plan width, ruling out very shallow (4:12) or steep (8:12+) slopes. Valley and hip intersection shadows show well-defined but not dramatic depth, further corroborating a 6:12 estimate. The southern Texas climate and architectural style of the neighborhood (multi-plane hip roofs on large suburban homes) are also strongly associated with 6:12 as the dominant pitch in this region.
footprint_rationale: Target home identified as the large, complex hip-roofed structure in the center of the image at approximately x:435–650, y:375–680 px. Bounding box measured at ~215 px wide × ~305 px deep (45.6 ft × 64.6 ft), giving a raw bounding area of ~2,943 sq ft. Applied a 75% fill factor to account for the numerous hip bays, valleys, and cutouts yielding ~2,207 sq ft footprint. The roof is entirely hip-style (no gable ends, so rakes = 0). Ridge lines are short horizontal peaks atop each hip section (~5 segments). Hip lines are numerous, running diagonally from each ridge end to each corner (~18 total). Valley lines are deep inward intersections between projecting wings and the main body (~8 total). Eaves run along the lower horizontal edges of each hip section (~14 segments). Confidence is moderate-low due to image complexity, multiple overlapping structures in dense development, and difficulty in precisely delineating individual unit boundaries.
